content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 15, 1991

Earl J. Cantos, Jr.
Deputy to Board Member
Ernest J. Dronenburg, Jr.
State Board of Equalization
110 West C Street, Suite 1709
San Diego, CA 92101

Re: Your Request For Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-259

Dear Mr. Cantos:

You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you on behalf of Board of Equalization Member Ernest J.
Dronenburg, Jr. and the entire State Board of Equalization¹
concerning the application of the mass mailing provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").² It appears your question is
hypothetical in nature at this time. Consequently, we are
treating your request as one for informal assistance.³

QUESTIONS

1. May the Board of Equalization mail 200 copies of its
annual report, which contains photographs and other references to

¹ In a telephone conversation with Mark Morodomi of this office
on June 11, 1991, you confirmed you were authorized to request the
advice on behalf of Board of Equalization Member Ernest J.
Dronenburg, Jr. and the entire State Board of Equalization.

² Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. Copies of
Commission regulations, advice letters and opinions may be
obtained upon request at a cost of 10¢ per page, plus postage.

³ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-91-259
Page 2


the five Board of Equalization members, in a single calendar month
without violating the mass mailing restriction of the Act?

    2. In addition to those mailed by the district, may each
member of the Board of Equalization send 200 copies of the annual
report in a single calendar month without violating the mass
mailing restrictions of the Act?

CONCLUSIONS

    1. Since a mailing of less than 201 substantially similar
pieces of mail in a calendar month is not a "mass mailing" under
the Act, such a mailing would not be subject to the mass mailing
restrictions of the Act.

    2. Each elected member of the Board of Equalization may send
200 copies of the annual report in a single month without the
mailings being cumulated for purposes of the mass mailing
restrictions of the Act, provided the member does not know or have
reason to know that the agency has also mailed 200 copies of the
report in the same calendar month or the member has coordinated
the mailing with other members.

FACTS

    The Board of Equalization (the "board") is made up of four
elected members, each representing a specified district in the
state, and one ex-officio member, the State Controller, who is
elected statewide.

    The board distributes a report on the state of the agency on
an annual basis. In addition, each individual Board member may
send out copies of the annual report independently from the
general agency mailing. You have indicated that the annual report
may include the signatures, pictures, and biographies of the board
members.

ANALYSIS

Mass Mailings Generally

    In June 1988, Proposition 73 amended Section 89001 of the Act
to provide: "No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at
public expense." This prohibition on certain publicly funded
mailings applies only to a "mass mailing" as defined in the Act.
"Mass mailing" means over two hundred substantially similar pieces
of mail, but does not include mail which is sent in response to an
unsolicited request, letter or other inquiry. (Section 82041.5;
Regulation 18901(a)(4), copy enclosed.) If less than 201 of the

Our File No. I-91-259
Page 3


same or substantially similar items are sent in a calendar month,
the regulation would not restrict the mailing.4

Moreover, items that are mailed in response to unsolicited
requests for information or mailings that fall within the
exceptions in Regulation 18901(b) are not counted toward the 201
that equate to a mass mailing. (Regulation 18901(c)(1).)

Finally, Regulation 18901(a) provides that a mailing is
prohibited only if all of the following apply:

(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means,
to the recipient at his or her residence, place of
employment or business, or post office box. For
purposes of this subdivision (a)(1), the item
delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item,
such as a videotape, record, or button, or a writ-
ten document.

(2) The item sent either:

(A) Features an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which produces or
sends the mailing, or

(B) Includes the name, office,
photograph, or other reference to an elected
officer affiliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing, and is prepared
or sent in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert with the elected
officer;

(3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is
paid for with public moneys; or

(B) Costs of design, production, and
printing exceeding $50.00 are paid with public
moneys, and the design, production, or


_________________________

4 Please note that even separate mailings may be counted together
if the items sent are substantially similar. "Substantially
similar" is defined in Regulation 18901(c)(3). Thus, if two
mailings, each of less than 200, are substantially similar, they
will be counted together to determine whether more than 200 items
are sent in a calendar month.

Our File No. I-91-259
Page 4


printing is done with the intent of sending
the item other than as permitted by this
regulation.

(4) More than two hundred substantially
similar items are sent, in a single calendar month,
excluding any item sent in response to an
unsolicited request and any item described in
subdivision (b);

Regulation 18901(a).

Thus, if less than 201 items are mailed or delivered to a
person's home, office or post office box, inclusion of the names,
photographs and signatures of elected officials is not
prohibited.5

Independent Mailings of the Board's Elected Members

Generally, the mailings of elected officers of an agency are
considered separately in applying the mass mailing restriction of
the Act and an elected officer is not required to monitor the
mailings of other elected officers of the agency. Thus, where
multiple elected members of an agency, such as the Legislature or
Board of Equalization, independently distribute 200 copies or less
of the same item, the mass mailing restriction of the Act would
not apply to those mailings.

However, this conclusion would be different if an elected
officer of an agency coordinates with other elected members of the
agency in order to circumvent the mass mailing restrictions of the
Act. Moreover, if the elected officer knows or has reason to know
that the agency has already mailed 200 copies of the report in the
same calendar month, the elected officer's mailing would be
cumulated with those of the agency with respect to the mass
mailing restrictions of the Act.

Thus, neither the agency or any elected officer of the agency
may coordinate the mailing of a single item to maximize it's
distribution.


_______________________________

5 The Commission's authority to interpret Section 89001 to avoid
the absurd results of its literal application was upheld in Watson
v. Fair Political Practices Com., (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 1059.

Our File No. I-91-259
Page 5


     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                   Sincerely,

                                   Scott Hallabrin
                                   Acting General Counsel

                                   By: John W. Wallace
                                       Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa

Enclosure

sections.facts:
The Board of Equalization (the "board") is made up of four elected members, each representing a specified district in the state, and one ex-official member, the State Controller, who is elected statewide.

The board distributes a report on the state of the agency on an annual basis. In addition, each individual Board member may send out copies of the annual report independently from the general agency mailing. You have indicated that the annual report may include the signatures, pictures, and biographies of the board members.
sections.analysis:
Mass Mailings Generally

In June 1988, Proposition 73 amended Section 89001 of the Act to provide: "No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at public expense." This prohibition on certain publicly funded mailings applies only to a "mass mailing" as defined in the Act. "Mass mailing" means over two hundred substantially similar pieces of mail, but does not include mail which is sent in response to an unsolicited request, letter or other inquiry. (Section 82041.5; Regulation 18901(a)(4), copy enclosed.) If less than 201 of the

same or substantially similar items are sent in a calendar month, the regulation would not restrict the mailing.4

Moreover, items that are mailed in response to unsolicited requests for information or mailings that fall within the exceptions in Regulation 18901(b) are not counted toward the 201 that equate to a mass mailing. (Regulation 18901(c)(4).)

Finally, Regulation 18901(a) provides that a mailing is prohibited only if all of the following apply:

(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means, to the recipient at his or her residence, place of employment or business, or post office box. For purposes of this subdivision (a)(1), the item delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item, such as a videotape, record, or button, or a written document.

(2) The item sent either:
    (A) Features an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing, or
    (B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing, and is prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected officer;

(3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with public moneys; or
    (B) Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $50.00 are paid with public moneys, and the design, production, or

4 Please note that even separate mailings may be counted together if the items sent are substantially similar. "Substantially similar" is defined in Regulation 18901(c)(3). Thus, if two mailings, each of less than 200, are substantially similar, they will be counted together to determine whether more than 200 items are sent in a calendar month.

printing is done with the intent of sending the item other than as permitted by this regulation.

(4) More than two hundred substantially similar items are sent, in a single calendar month, excluding any item sent in response to an unsolicited request and any item described in subdivision (b);

Regulation 18901(a).

Thus, if less than 201 items are mailed or delivered to a person's home, office or post office box, inclusion of the names, photographs and signatures of elected officials is not prohibited.5

Independent Mailings of the Board's Elected Members

Generally, the mailings of elected officers of an agency are considered separately in applying the mass mailing restriction of the Act and an elected officer is not required to monitor the mailings of other elected officers of the agency. Thus, where multiple elected members of an agency, such as the Legislature or Board of Equalization, independently distribute 200 copies or less of the same item, the mass mailing restriction of the Act would not apply to those mailings.

However, this conclusion would be different if an elected officer of an agency coordinates with other elected members of the agency in order to circumvent the mass mailing restrictions of the Act. Moreover, if the elected officer knows or has reason to know that the agency has already mailed 200 copies of the report in the same calendar month, the elected officer's mailing would be cumulated with those of the agency with respect to the mass mailing restrictions of the Act.

Thus, neither the agency or any elected officer of the agency may coordinate the mailing of a single item to maximize it's distribution.

5 The Commission's authority to interpret Section 89001 to avoid the absurd results of its literal application was upheld in Watson v. Fair Political Practices Com., (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 1059.
